output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.5, "R1.3": 0.3, "R1.4": 0.4, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.5}}

Summary: Reframed the introduction around a single clearly-articulated contribution (the first sublinear regret bound for exposure-constrained ranking), demoting the empirical results to supporting evidence and removing the unsupported "benchmark suite" framing that conflates an artifact release with a scientific contribution.

Diagnosis of the current framing
The passage tries to carry three contributions at once — an algorithm (FairCPO), a theorem (O(√T) regret), and a benchmark/leaderboard — and each one undercuts the others. The empirical paragraph leads, which signals "another fairness-LTR method," but the second paragraph then says the *real* contribution is theoretical. The third paragraph escalates further, declaring the work "a benchmark suite" and prescribing that future work must report on it — a claim a single paper cannot legitimately make on its own behalf, and one that distracts from the genuinely novel theoretical result. The story should be inverted: the regret bound is the conceptual anchor; the algorithm is the constructive object that realizes it; the experiments and released artifacts are evidence and infrastructure.

A second framing problem: the current text describes *what* FairCPO does (primal-dual, trust region, Lagrangian) before it has told the reader *why the problem is hard* or what was missing from prior work. Exposure-constrained ranking already has a substantial literature; the reader needs to know in the first paragraph what specifically was open and why closing it required a new technique (the slate-action adaptation of constrained-MDP analysis), not just that "prior work established only asymptotic convergence."

Revised text

> Exposure-fairness constraints in learning-to-rank have, until now, admitted only asymptotic guarantees: existing constrained rankers converge to a feasible policy in the limit, but offer no finite-time control over either utility regret or constraint violation. This gap matters in practice — deployed rankers operate at finite horizons, and a guarantee that vanishes "eventually" leaves open how much utility is sacrificed, and how badly exposure can drift, along the way.
>
> We close this gap. Our central result is an O(√T) regret bound, against the best feasible stochastic policy in hindsight, for exposure-constrained ranking under bounded relevance and a Slater-type condition on the exposure envelope. To our knowledge this is the first sublinear regret guarantee in this setting. The proof lifts a recent line of analysis from constrained Markov decision processes to the slate-action structure of ranked retrieval, where the action space is combinatorial in the slate size and the standard CMDP arguments do not apply directly; the technical core of the paper is this lifting.
>
> To make the bound constructive we introduce FairCPO, a primal-dual algorithm with a trust-region step on the policy parameters that instantiates the analysis. FairCPO treats the per-query ranker as a stochastic policy over permutations and constrains the expected exposure of each protected group to a prescribed envelope around its population proportion. On MSLR-WEB30K, Yahoo! LTR Set 1, and Istella-S, FairCPO improves NDCG@10 by 1.4 to 2.7 points over the strongest fairness-constrained baseline at matched exposure-violation budgets, confirming that the regret-bounded design is not won at the cost of empirical utility.
>
> To support replication and follow-on theoretical work, we release the three corpora repackaged with an exposure-violation evaluation harness, reference implementations of seven prior fairness-constrained rankers, and a submission server that reports NDCG and exposure-deviation curves on held-out queries.

What changed and why
- The opening now states the gap (no finite-time guarantees) before naming the method, so the reader understands what FairCPO is *for* before learning what it *is*.
- The regret bound is promoted to the lede and the algorithm is reintroduced as the constructive vehicle for the theorem, inverting the original ordering.
- The "benchmark suite" paragraph and its self-prescriptive recommendation ("future work should report on the FairCPO suite … to ensure comparability with the regret-bounded frontier we establish") have been removed. A single paper cannot anoint its own release as a community benchmark, and the prescription reads as overreach that will draw reviewer fire without strengthening the contribution. The released artifacts are retained but reframed as replication infrastructure, which is what they actually are at submission time.
- The novelty claim is sharpened from "first sublinear regret guarantee for any exposure-constrained ranker" to the same claim with explicit scope ("in this setting") and a one-sentence justification of *why* the lifting is non-trivial (combinatorial slate actions break standard CMDP arguments). Without that justification the novelty claim reads as a plug-in application.
- "Mirroring the structure of constrained policy optimization in continuous control" was dropped; it invites the reader to ask what is genuinely new versus CPO, and the answer (the slate-action lifting) is now stated directly.

Unverified items reviewers will likely probe
- Whether the O(√T) bound actually holds uniformly in the slate size, or whether constants blow up combinatorially — the revised text implies the lifting is the hard part, so the proof had better address this explicitly in the body.
- Whether prior exposure-constrained rankers truly offer *only* asymptotic guarantees, or whether any recent work (e.g., bandit-style fair ranking with regret analyses) already provides finite-time bounds of some form. The "first sublinear regret guarantee" claim should be checked against the fair-bandits literature before final submission.
- The "1.4 to 2.7 point" NDCG@10 improvement is reproduced from the original artifact and not independently verified.
